actions.thought: Holding 5c 4s on the button in a heads-up situation, I have the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. However, 54o is a very weak starting hand with low equity and poor playability. While I could attempt a raise to steal the blinds, against an opponent labeled as 'Pro', I prefer to start conservatively and not overplay marginal junk hands in the very first hand of the tournament. Folding here is a disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

In a heads-up match, holding Ace-Eight offsuit on the Button is a strong starting hand, well above the median. I have position on the opponent for the rest of the hand. Raising is the standard play here to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take the pot down immediately or build it while holding the best hand and position. A minimum raise to 200 is sufficient to achieve these goals while managing risk.

Holding Kc 4s in the Big Blind, I am facing a min-raise to 200 from the Small Blind. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call (100 to win 300). While Kc 4s is a relatively weak hand and I will be out of position post-flop, it possesses sufficient equity against a wide SB opening range to justify defending my blind and seeing a flop in a deep-stacked scenario.

I am first to act on the flop after defending my Big Blind. The board is 8c-5s-Th, which doesn't particularly favor my Kc 4s. Since I have no pair and no real draw, checking is the standard play to see how the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) chooses to proceed. I will decide my next move based on their bet sizing or if they choose to check behind.

I hold middle pair (Eights) with the top kicker (Ace) on a T-8-5 rainbow board. While this is a decent hand in heads-up play, the presence of the Ten as an overcard makes it vulnerable to hands in the Big Blind's defending range (like Tx). Betting here isolates me against better hands (Tx, two pair, sets) and folds out worse hands that have little equity, though it does get protection against overcards. However, checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially induce bluffs from the opponent on later streets. It avoids the difficult situation of facing a check-raise. This is a classic 'pot control' spot with a marginal made hand.

On the turn card 7s, the board is 8c-5s-Th-7s. I currently have King-high with no draws and no showdown value. The flop went check-check, suggesting my opponent likely has a marginal hand or is also giving up. However, since I have no equity and am out of position, betting here as a pure bluff is risky. Checking is the most sensible play to see a free river or fold if faced with a bet, as I have no reason to commit more chips with air.

The turn card is the Seven of Spades (7s), which puts a flush draw on the board and completes potential straights (specifically 9-6). While I hold middle pair with a top kicker, the board texture has become wetter and more dangerous. Betting here provides protection against overcards but risks isolating me against better hands like a Ten, two pair, or a made straight, and opens me up to a difficult check-raise. Checking back is the prudent play to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially induce bluffs from the opponent on the river. I want to get to showdown cheaply rather than bloating the pot with a marginal made hand.

The board has run out 8c-5s-Th-7s-Jh. I have King-high with no showdown value. After defending the big blind pre-flop, both players have checked the flop and the turn. This suggests that the opponent (Pro) likely has a marginal hand with some showdown value or has given up on the pot. While I could attempt a bluff, the board texture is quite connected, and a small bet might be called by any pair. Given the small pot size and the fact that it's early in the tournament, I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown or fold if faced with a bet, as King-high is unlikely to be the best hand here.

On the River, the board is [8c 5s Th 7s Jh]. My hand is [Ah 8d], giving me a pair of Eights. The board is very coordinated; any 9 makes a straight (7-8-9-T-J), and there are now two overcards to my pair (Ten and Jack). The opponent has checked to me three times. Betting has very little value here because better hands (Jack, Ten, Straight, Two Pair) will call or raise, and worse hands (lower pairs, Ace-high) will likely fold. Checking back guarantees I see a showdown for free, giving me the chance to win the pot if the opponent holds a weak hand like a small pair or missed overcards, while avoiding losing more chips to a trap or a better hand.

I am on the button with Ace-Five offsuit. This is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative, put pressure on the big blind, and build a pot with my Ace-high equity.

I am in the Big Blind with K7s, facing a 3x open from the Button (Flash). In heads-up play, K7s is well within the defending range given its flush potential and high card value. I need to call 200 chips to win a pot that will be 600, requiring 33% equity. Against a standard button opening range, K7s performs well enough to justify a call. Although I will be out of position post-flop, folding is too tight here. I choose to flat call to realize my equity.

I have flopped Trip Kings on a very dry board (K-6-K). In this heads-up scenario, this is a monster hand. Leading out (donk betting) would likely cause my opponent to fold all their air, which constitutes a large portion of their wide button-opening range. I want to keep their bluffs in and give them a chance to catch up or fire a continuation bet. Checking is the standard line here to maximize value over multiple streets by inducing aggression or keeping the pot manageable until the river if they check back.

On a paired board of King-King-Six, my Ace-high has significant showdown value in a heads-up pot. Since the opponent checked, I will check back to control the pot size and realize my equity. Betting here might only get called by better hands (like a King or a pocket pair) or force out hands that I already beat. Checking allows me to see the turn for free and potentially catch an Ace or simply reach showdown.

I have turned a Full House (Kings over Sixes) on a double-paired board (K-K-6-6). While this is a very strong hand, my kicker (7) is relatively weak if the opponent also holds a King. Since the opponent checked back the flop, their range is polarized between air/weak hands and slow-played monsters (like a better King). If they have air, betting now likely causes them to fold, gaining me nothing. If they have a better King, betting builds a pot I will lose. Checking is the optimal play: it induces bluffs from their weak hands and keeps the pot manageable in case I am out-kicked. I will check to the opponent.

The board has double-paired with Kings and Sixes (Kc 6d Ks 6s). My hand is effectively the best possible hand (K-K-6-6-A) unless the opponent has a King or a Six for a full house. Since the opponent checked both the flop and the turn, I will check back to control the pot size and realize my Ace-high value. There is little value in betting as I am either way ahead of their non-Ace hands or way behind a slow-played monster.

I hold a Full House (Kings full of Sixes) on a K-K-6-6-Q board. Given the double-paired board, any King the opponent holds results in a split pot (unless they hold K-Q for a better boat, or K-6 for quads, which is unlikely given the action). The opponent checked back both the Flop and Turn, suggesting they lack a King and likely hold a pocket pair, Ace-high, or a Queen. If I bet, I force them to fold the hands I beat (Ace-high, low pairs), and I only get called by hands that chop or beat me (K-x, K-Q). Checking is the optimal play to induce bluffs from their air or missed draws, as my triple-check line looks like a weak give-up. If they check back, I likely win the showroom or chop.

The board is double-paired with Kings and Sixes (Kc 6d Ks 6s) and a Queen on the river (Qc). My hand is K-K-6-6-A. Pro has checked three times. Since my hand has high showdown value with an Ace kicker on this board, I will check back. Betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I would only be called or raised by hands that beat me (like a Queen, Six, or King). Checking ensures I realize my equity in this pot.

I am in the Small Blind / Button position in a Heads-Up match with [As 4s]. This is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, ranking well within the top range of hands I should be playing. A suited Ace has good playability post-flop and blocks opponents' Ax hands. Given my opponent has shown a tendency to check down hands, I want to take the initiative. A min-raise to 200 is a standard and effective play to build the pot in position and potentially take it down pre-flop or define ranges.

I am in the Big Blind with 7-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. The opponent has raised to 200 (a min-raise). While the pot odds are 3:1 to call, 7-4 offsuit realizes its equity poorly out of position. I have 146 hands remaining in the tournament, so I prefer to fold this garbage hand and wait for better spots rather than playing a weak hand out of position against a competent opponent.

QTo is a strong starting hand in a heads-up match, falling within the top tier of hands to open from the button. I want to build a pot while I have positional advantage. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard aggressive play that remains consistent with my sizing in Hand #3.

I am in the Big Blind holding KTs (King-Ten suited), which is a very strong hand in heads-up play, typically well ahead of a Button's opening range. My opponent raised to 300 (3bb). I am getting 3:1 pot odds, requiring only 25% equity to call, while KTs has roughly 55% equity against a standard opening range. Since I will be out of position post-flop, flat calling is prudent to keep the pot manageable and keep my opponent's range wide (including hands I dominate like K9, QT, etc.), rather than 3-betting and potentially facing a 4-bet or folding out worse hands. I will call the additional 200 to match the 300 total bet.

On a paired board of Jc-3h-Jd, standard strategy for the Big Blind defender is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Although I have decent equity with King-high and backdoor draws (flush and straight), leading out (donk betting) is not optimal as it allows the opponent to play perfectly against my range. I will check to see if the opponent continues or checks back.

The flop is Jc-3h-Jd, a very dry, paired board. As the pre-flop aggressor, this board texture is generally better for my range than for the caller's. I have Queen-high, which has some showdown value but would benefit from folding out the opponent's air or low pocket pairs. A small continuation bet of 200 into the 600 pot (1/3 pot) is a standard play to maintain initiative and capitalize on the board texture.

On this dry, paired board (J-J-3), the opponent's c-bet of 1/3 pot (200 into 600) is standard with a very wide range, including many hands that missed completely. Holding KhTh, I have King-high which is currently ahead of many of the opponent's semi-bluffs or lower high-card hands. Additionally, I have a backdoor flush draw (hearts) and backdoor straight possibilities. Getting 5:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 1000), I only need roughly 17-20% equity to continue. Against a wide button c-betting range, my hand has significantly more equity than that. Folding here would be over-folding and exploitable. Raising turns my hand into a bluff unnecessarily when I have showdown value. Therefore, calling to re-evaluate on the turn is the optimal play.

On the turn, the board is Jc-3h-Jd-9h. This is a fantastic card for my hand (KhTh). I have picked up a King-high flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (a Queen makes a straight), in addition to my King-high showdown value which might still be good against pure bluffs. Despite the increased equity, leading out (donk betting) is generally inferior to checking here. Checking keeps the opponent's range wide, allowing them to bluff with air or check back a marginal hand. If I bet, I likely fold out hands I am beating and get called mostly by trips or better made hands. I will check to the aggressor and evaluate their action.

I have picked up an open-ended straight draw (any King or Eight) on the turn to go with my two overcards. Pro has checked to me again. Given the flop action where Pro called a continuation bet on a dry, paired board, they likely have some showdown value like a small pocket pair or Ace-high. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free and see the river without bloating the pot with a draw.

On the river, the board is Jc-3h-Jd-9h-2d. I missed my flush and straight draws, leaving me with King-high. The opponent checked back the turn, which often indicates a capped range consisting of marginal showdown value (like Ace-high or small pairs) or a complete give-up. While my King-high beats their give-ups (like Queen-high), I lose to all their Ace-highs and pairs. Leading out as a bluff here is risky because the 2d is a brick that doesn't change the board texture, and opponents frequently look up river leads with Ace-high or bottom pair after checking the turn. Checking is the prudent play: it gives me a chance to win at showdown against worse air if they check back, while allowing me to easily fold if they bet, preserving my stack early in the tournament.

The river 2d is a complete brick, and I am left with Queen-high. Pro called my continuation bet on the paired flop and has since checked both the turn and the river. This line suggests Pro has a marginal hand with some showdown value, such as Ace-high or a small pocket pair, and is looking to get to showdown cheaply. While my Queen-high will rarely win at showdown, it does beat a few total air hands. Turning my hand into a bluff here is risky because the turn went check-check, which weakens my perceived range; Pro might be inclined to 'hero call' with Ace-high or a small pair. I will check back to minimize my losses and move to the next hand.

Current stack depth is 111 BB. I am on the Button (SB) with 6d 5s, an unsuited connector. While Button opening ranges in heads-up are wide, 65o falls into the bottom percentiles of starting hands and is typically a fold in standard GTO charts (whereas 65s might be a raise or limp). My opponent (Flash) has folded the Big Blind once to a raise but also defended once. Flash plays somewhat passively post-flop. While attempting a steal is tempting given the fold equity and position, 65o has poor playability if called and can lead to dominated situations. Conserving chips and tightening the range slightly here is a prudent play to avoid high-variance spots with weak holdings.

I have 6h7h on the button, which is a strong suited connector for heads-up play. I want to take the initiative and build the pot while in position. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size that is consistent with my previous betting patterns in this tournament.

I hold Pocket Sixes (6s 6c) in the Big Blind. My opponent (Flash) has raised to 300 (3bb) from the Button. In heads-up play, 66 is a strong hand, usually a favorite against a standard opening range. I have excellent pot odds to call (need to put in 200 to win a pot that will be 600, requiring 33% equity, while 66 has ~60% equity against a wide range). While a 3-bet is an option to deny equity to overcards, calling is a lower-variance line that keeps the opponent's range wide and manages the pot size while playing out of position. I will call the raise and evaluate the flop.

I hold Pocket Sixes (6s 6c) on a board of [3h Th 2s]. This is a decent flop for my hand, as there is only one overcard (the Ten). In a heads-up scenario, my hand is currently ahead of a large portion of the opponent's opening range, which includes many unpaired overcards (e.g., KQ, AJ, Q9) and missed flush draws. Standard play out of position against the pre-flop aggressor is to check. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get action from better hands (Tx or higher pairs) and fold out the hands I beat. By checking, I allow the opponent to make a continuation bet with his bluffs or check back, keeping the pot manageable. If he bets, I plan to call given my showdown value and the texture of the board.

I have a flush draw with 6h7h on a 3hTh2s board. Having the initiative from pre-flop and being in position, a continuation bet is the standard play here. This allows me to take down the pot immediately if my opponent missed, or build the pot for a potential flush. A half-pot bet of 300 is balanced and puts pressure on the opponent's marginal holdings.

I hold Pocket Sixes (6s 6c) on a [3h Th 2s] board. The opponent has c-bet 300 into a pot of 600. This is a half-pot bet, requiring me to have 25% equity to call profitably. My hand is ahead of all unpaired overcard hands (like KQ, AJ, QJ) which make up a significant portion of a wide button opening range. I only trail against a Ten, an overpair, or a set. Raising would isolate me against better hands, while folding is too weak given the board texture and my holding. Calling allows me to keep the opponent's bluffing range wide and re-evaluate on the turn.

The turn card is the Ace of Spades (As), making the board [3h Th 2s As]. This is a significant scare card that hits the opponent's pre-flop raising range (which includes many Ax hands) much harder than my check-calling range. My Pocket Sixes are now counterfeit against any Ace and still lose to any Ten. Leading out (donk betting) would accomplish nothing as worse hands fold and better hands call. The standard line is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If the opponent bets, I will likely have to fold, as the Ace is a perfect card for them to barrel, whether they hit it or are bluffing, and my hand is too weak to call a double barrel on this texture.

The Ace of Spades on the turn is a significant scare card that hits my perceived range as the pre-flop raiser. While I could bet to represent the Ace and potentially force a fold from a Ten or a small pair, checking back allows me to realize my equity and see the river for free. Since I have a flush draw (9 outs), I have a good chance of improving, and checking keeps the pot manageable if I miss. If I hit my heart on the river, I can then value bet effectively.

I hold Pocket Sixes on a board of [3h Th 2s As Jh]. The river completes a potential heart flush and puts three broadway cards on the board (T, A, J). My hand is very weak, beating only missed straight draws or total air. The opponent checked back the turn (an Ace), which often indicates a made hand with showdown value (like a weak Ace or Ten) exercising pot control, or a complete give-up. Since the river card is the Jack of Hearts, it interacts heavily with the board, completing flushes and straights. Leading out as a bluff is risky because the opponent's check-back range on the turn likely contains many hands that catch a piece of this board or are already ahead (Ax). Checking is the standard line to try and get to showdown cheaply against missed draws, though I expect to lose to most of the opponent's range. If the opponent bets, I will fold.

I completed my flush on the river with the Jack of Hearts. Pro has checked twice since the Ace appeared on the turn, suggesting they likely have a marginal made hand like a Ten, an Ace, or a Jack that they are looking to take to showdown cheaply. Since I have a very strong hand, I must bet for value. A bet of 800 into the 1200 pot (2/3 pot) is a standard sizing that targets those mid-strength hands while still appearing balanced.

On the River, the board is [3h Th 2s As Jh]. The pot is 1200, and the opponent bets 800 (2/3 pot). I hold [6s 6c], which is now a very weak hand given the board texture. There are three overcards (T, A, J) and three hearts, making straights and flushes possible. The opponent checked back the Turn (As), which often indicates a medium-strength hand (like a weak Ace or Ten) exercising pot control or a draw. The River bet of 800 is polarizing; it represents a strong made hand (Flush, Straight, Two Pair) or a bluff. While the opponent could be bluffing with missed spades or air, my hand beats almost nothing in their value range. Additionally, I have many better hands in my range to call with (such as Ax, Tx, or a flush). Calling with 66 requires the opponent to be bluffing very frequently, which is a high-risk assumption on this wet board. The pot odds of ~28.5% are not compelling enough for a hero call with the bottom of my range. Folding is the correct play.

In Heads-Up play, J8o on the button is a playable hand, typically falling into a raising or limping range. Since I have position and the opponent (Flash) has shown passivity when defending the Big Blind (Hand #2 checked down), a min-raise serves to steal the blind or build a pot where I have a positional advantage. I will raise to 200 to apply pressure.

I am holding a pocket pair (33), which is a strong hand in heads-up play. I will 3-bet to 600 to take the initiative and build the pot, while also giving myself the chance to win the hand immediately if my opponent folds. If called, I have set-mining potential and showdown value on low-card boards.

I opened with J8o from the Button, which is a standard steal attempt, but the opponent has 3-bet to 600. This is the first time the opponent has 3-bet from the Big Blind in this match, suggesting a stronger range or a deliberate aggression adjustment. J8o performs poorly against a standard 3-betting range (dominated by AJ, KJ, QJ, and pocket pairs). Although I would have position post-flop, the pot odds of 33% are not sufficient to justify calling with a hand that likely has only ~30% equity and poor playability. Folding is the prudent decision to preserve chips.

I am on the button with 8d 5s. While this is a weak hand, in heads-up play, maintaining aggression from the button is important. I've noticed Pro has folded to pressure in previous hands. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to try and take the blinds or set up a play on the flop.

I hold 6h 3d, which is a very weak offsuit unconnected hand. Although the pot odds are tempting (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 600), my equity against a standard button opening range is poor (around 30%), and the hand has very poor playability out of position. Against an opponent who is opening to 3x (300) rather than a min-raise, defending with trash hands like 63o is a losing strategy long-term due to reverse implied odds.

I hold 8d 3d in the Small Blind. This is a very weak hand (bottom 15-20% of hands). While heads-up strategy generally requires playing a wide range from the button, 83s is on the cusp of unplayable, often dominated and difficult to play post-flop. In Hand 8, the opponent 3-bet my button raise, indicating they are defending their Big Blind aggressively. Opening with a marginal hand like this invites a 3-bet that I cannot call, or a flat call where I am out of position with weak equity. I folded 56o in a similar spot (Hand 6), and 83s is comparably weak. Folding is the disciplined, low-variance play here to preserve the chip lead.

Holding A5o on the button in a heads-up situation is a standard opening hand. Based on my previous play in this tournament (Hands #3, #5, #7, and #9), I have consistently opened for 300 from the small blind when entering the pot. This sizing allows me to maintain consistency, exert pressure on the big blind, and likely take the pot down pre-flop or play a pot in position with an Ace-high hand.

The opponent has raised to 300 (3x BB) from the button, consistent with their previous aggressive opening strategy. In a heads-up match, ranges are wide, and T6s (Ten-Six suited) is a playable hand with reasonable equity (~40-45%) against a standard button opening range. The pot odds require me to call 200 into a pot of 400 (33% equity needed), which justifies a defend. Although being out of position is a disadvantage, the suited nature of the hand allows for equity realization. Folding here would be too tight given the wide ranges.

On this flop of 2s Ah Ts, I have flopped middle pair with the Ten of Diamonds. The board texture contains an Ace, which favors the pre-flop raiser's range significantly. Being out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get called by better hands (Ax, better Tx) and fold out worse ones, denying me the opportunity to induce bluffs or keep the pot manageable. I intend to check and evaluate my opponent's action; middle pair is strong enough to consider calling a standard continuation bet, but checking is the requisite first step.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace on a 2s-Ah-Ts board. While my kicker is weak, I likely have the best hand at this stage. Pro checked, and I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to extract value from draws, middle pairs, or weaker Aces, while also denying equity to various hands in Pro's range.

I hold middle pair (Tens) on an Ace-high board. The opponent's continuation bet of half-pot (300 into 600) is a standard play on this texture, as the Ace favors their range. However, in heads-up play, ranges are wide, and the opponent will be c-betting this board with a high frequency, including flush draws, straight draws, and air. I need 20% equity to call (300 to win 1500 total pot). My hand is too strong to fold to a single bet, as I beat all bluffs and some lower pairs. Raising would isolate me against better hands. The correct play is to call and re-evaluate on the turn.

On the Turn, the board is [2s Ah Ts Th]. The [Th] is an excellent card for me, improving my hand to Trip Tens (Trips). The pot is 1200. I am out of position. My opponent showed aggression pre-flop and on the flop. If I bet out (donk bet) now, I risk folding out my opponent's bluffs (air, missed draws) that might fire a second barrel. If the opponent has a strong hand like an Ace or a full house, they will likely bet for value. If they have a draw, they might bet as a semi-bluff. Therefore, checking is the best option to extract maximum value, either by inducing a bluff or allowing the opponent to value bet a worse hand (like Ax).

On the turn, the board pairs the Ten (2s-Ah-Ts-Th). I have top pair with a weak kicker (A5). Pro has checked to me again. While I likely still have the best hand, the paired Ten increases the risk that Pro has improved to trips if they were defending with a hand like JT, QT, or KT. By checking back here, I control the size of the pot, avoid a potential check-raise, and can potentially induce a bluff on the river or get a value bet called by a wider range of hands (like mid-pairs or missed draws) on the final street.

On the River, the board is [2s Ah Ts Th Kd]. I hold [6d Td] for Trip Tens with Ace-King kickers (playing the board's Ace and King). The opponent checked back the Turn, which suggests they likely do not have a monster (like a full house or strong trips) as they would likely bet for value or protection on a draw-heavy board. Their range likely consists of Ax (top pair on flop), Kx (rivered pair), missed flush draws, or a straight draw (QJ) that got there. Checking the river risks the opponent checking back Ax or Kx for showdown value. A value bet targets these specific hands. I lose to QJ (straight) and boats (KT, AT, AA, KK), but the turn check discounts the strongest hands. I will bet just under half pot to extract value from Ax and Kx.

I have top pair (Aces) with a weak kicker. The board has paired Tens and a King arrived on the river. Pro led for 500 into a 1200 pot after the turn went check-check. This sizing (approx. 42% pot) could represent a King trying to see where it stands, a weak Ace, or a missed draw (like spades) turning into a bluff. While it's possible Pro has a Ten or a better Ace, the price to call (500 to win a total pot of 2200) is very favorable at 4.4 to 1. In a heads-up match, folding top pair to a sub-half-pot bet after checking the turn would be overly exploitable. I will call to see the showdown.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with Ten-Five offsuit (T5o). This is a weak hand with poor playability and low equity against a random hand. In previous hands from this position (Hand #6 with 56o, Hand #10 with 83o), I have folded weak holdings. My opponent has shown aggression (3-betting in Hand #8), making it risky to open with marginal hands. With a chip lead (11200 vs 8650), conserving chips by folding the bottom of my range is a prudent strategy.

I am on the button with 6c 3c. In heads-up play, this hand is strong enough to open for a raise to maintain pressure and exploit position post-flop. I have consistently raised to 300 in previous orbits from this position, so I will continue with that sizing to remain balanced and aggressive.

I am in the Big Blind holding 6s 8d. The opponent (Button) has raised to 300 (3bb). I need to call 200 more into a pot of 400, giving me 2:1 pot odds (33% equity required). Against a 3x open, 86o is a very marginal hand. It suffers from poor playability out of position and will likely under-realize its raw equity. While I have defended against 3x opens previously with stronger hands (suited connectors, pairs, broadways), 86o is too weak to profitably call a larger raise size like this. Folding preserves chips for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with J2o, which is a very weak hand, sitting near the bottom of the heads-up starting hand range. Although playing from the button requires a wide opening frequency, J2o is often outside the profitable opening range, especially against an opponent who has demonstrated the willingness to 3-bet (as seen in Hand #8). Folding here prevents getting involved in a marginal spot with poor playability post-flop.

In this heads-up situation, I am on the button with a weak hand, 92o. While normally I would fold such a hand to avoid getting into trouble, in a heads-up game I should occasionally exert pressure or simply fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities. Given the recent history and the fact that 92o is very weak, folding is a standard and safe play to avoid losing more chips with a marginal hand out of position post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Nine suited (Ah 9h), which is a very strong hand in heads-up play (top 10-15% of hands). My standard strategy from the button is to raise to steal the blinds or build value. Raising to 200 (2BB) is consistent with my previous play and puts pressure on the Big Blind while building a pot with a hand that plays well post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with T6o. Pro has raised to 2x (200) from the Button. I only need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 pot odds. In heads-up play, T6o is generally strong enough to defend the Big Blind against a wide opening range at these odds, despite being out of position. I will call to see the flop.

I have Tc6h on a 7h 5h 9s flop, giving me an inside straight draw (gutshot) to an 8. Since I am in the Big Blind and was the caller pre-flop, I will follow standard play and check to the pre-flop raiser. This allows me to see how the opponent reacts to the board texture before committing more chips.

I have flopped Top Pair Top Kicker (Nines with Ace kicker) combined with the Nut Flush Draw on a wet board of [7h 5h 9s]. This is an exceptionally strong hand with both immediate showdown value and high potential to improve to the nuts. Given the wet texture of the board (straight draws and flush possibilities), checking back is risky as many turn cards could kill the action or downgrade my relative hand strength.
